observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the GSO to compare the latest RPCPPE with the PPELCs and prepare a list of PPE items recorded in the PPELCs but not included in the RPCPPE, as well as PPE items included in the RPCPPE but not recorded in the PPELCs. This should be done prior to conducting the inventory, in accordance with Paragraph 6.1 of COA Circular No. 2020-006, to easily identify the items not recorded in either set of records. The GSO should reconcile the inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records and prepare the required lists or reports to be submitted to the Accounting Office for recording in the books pursuant to Paragraph 6.3 of the same circular, to establish the correct balances of PPE accounts.
observations.managementAction: The comparison of GSO and Accounting Office's records are partially done prior to the conduct of inventory due to the voluminous assets of the City. The GSO and the Accounting Office are currently conducting meetings and reconciliation. The reconciliation is still on-going.
observations.reason: The reconciliation is still pending due to the voluminous assets of the city.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the City Treasurer to provide the City Accountant with the certified list of taxpayers and taxes due based on the RPTAR at the beginning of each year. This list will be used by the City Accountant to set-up RPT and SET receivables during the year pursuant to Section 20, Volume 1 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, to ensure reliable balances of the said accounts in the financial statements as of year-end.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer provided the list of taxpayers and taxes due based on the RPTAR to the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: The Accounting Office used the RPT and SET receivables generated by the e-NGAS to set up these accounts in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC and the BAC Secretariat to ensure submission of copies of all contracts with the corresponding supporting documents to the Auditor within five working days from the execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001. We also recommended that Management forfeit the bid security or enforce the bid securing declaration if the bidder is unable to sign the contract and submit the documents that form part of the contract within 10 calendar days from receipt of NOA.
observations.managementAction: Copies of some contracts were submitted by the BAC to the Audit Team after receiving them back from the contractors. The BAC conducted follow-ups with contractors who have not submitted their documents.
observations.reason: Delays had been incurred in the submission of contracts that were bid during the year. Not all contracts were submitted to the Office of the Auditor within the reglementary period. No bid securities were forfeited. However, it was decided to forfeit the bid security of the bidders who are still in delay after sending warning letters.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the City Treasurer and City Accountant to release the RPT share for the 42 barangays within five days after the end of each quarter, in accordance with Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160. This would provide the barangays with additional funds for the implementation of their PPAs.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer's Office has transmitted the Reports of Collections and Deposits to the Accounting Office as basis for the computation of RPT shares.
observations.reason: The RPT shares for each quarter were released to the respective barangays beyond the reglementary period, as follows: 1st and 2nd quarter - October 30, 2024; 3rd quarter - November 15, 2024; 4th quarter - February 6, 2025.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the CHRMO to immediately update the membership profile of the employees to reflect the new salary rate, employment status, position or any other important information by submitting the required forms to the GSIS and PHIC and furnish copy of these forms to the Accounting Office. We also recommended that Management direct the Accounting Office to determine employees with unremitted contributions and overpayments and subsequently remit payments already due to GSIS, HDMF and PHIC and/or refund erroneously withheld payments to employees.
observations.managementAction: The CHRMO already updated the membership profile of the employees. The accounting personnel is doing his best in determining the discrepancies noted. The Accounting Office prepared the necessary adjusting journal entry. The DRRMF received by the City was reclassified under JEV No. 2024-01-000288 dated January 2, 2024.
observations.reason: There were still unreconciled balances in the Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-ibig and Due to Philhealth accounts as of December 31, 2024. The remittance/refund of premiums has not yet been processed, as the reconciliation is still partially completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the HoA instruct the City Accountant to: a. Submit the supporting documents for the grant of the said cash advance to the Office of the Auditor in a sealed envelope with a visible label 'CONFIDENTIAL – For the COA ATL Only' and with a covering transmittal letter detailing the submissions; and b. Restore the cash advance in the books by debiting ₱100,000.00 to Advances to Special Disbursing Officer and crediting Prior Period Adjustment in the same amount; and c. Record the liquidation of the cash advance in the books upon receipt of the transmittal letter duly received by the ICFAU staff.
observations.managementAction: Securing 'receiving copy' and other relevant documentations from recipients is ongoing. Adjusting entries were recorded in the books. Securing 'receiving copy' and other relevant documentations from recipients is still on-going.
observations.reason: No documents were submitted to the Office of the Auditor. Securing 'receiving copy' and other relevant documentations from recipients is ongoing; hence, the liquidation was recorded prior to submission and no documents were submitted to COA. No adjusting entry was made initially but later adjusting entries were recorded under JEV No. 2024-01-005713 dated January 2, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the implementing offices to prepare the Program of Works (POW), technical specifications and other necessary documents prior to the inclusion of these PPAs in the budget to avoid delays in the implementation of projects. We recommended that Management require the CPDC, in coordination with other implementing offices, to prepare a monitoring report for all projects of the City to ensure that all development projects of the City are implemented in accordance with the approved budgets.
observations.managementAction: To complete the POW and plans of the required projects unimplemented and will be subjected to implementation. Some claimants were assisted in the procurement of their supporting documents. Coordinated with related offices. Quarterly Report of the CDF utilization was uploaded in the FDP Portal, as well as Civil Works and goods being implemented. They are also posted in 3 conspicuous places (City Hall, CHO and Public Market).
observations.reason: On-going implementation due to lack of technical personnel involved in the preparation of POWs and plans. The budget realignment took some hearings under the SP committee and waiting for the approval. There is lack of technical personnel involved in the preparation of POWs and plans. Some projects remain unimplemented as of December 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the CDRRMC to decide on the use of the unexpended balance of the donations which shall be incorporated in the DRRMF Investment Plan to finance programs or projects for the disaster risk management activities of the City.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office prepared the necessary journal entry. The DRRMF received by the City was reclassified under JEV No. 2024-01-000288 dated January 2, 2024. The CDRRMO initially proposed where the fund would be used but the proposal was not finalized and was not included in the DRRMF Investment Plan.
observations.reason: The CDRRMO requested a realignment, including the specific projects and activities to which the funds will be allocated; however, the Office has not yet finalized the proposal and the revised DRRMF Investment Plan has not been included/approved. Only part of the fund was utilized and an amount of ₱973,750.00 has been utilized for purchase of assorted supplies and materials for the evacuation center; not all funds were used to finance programs or projects of CDRRMC.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: a. Instruct the CGSO to adjust the RPCPPE by effecting the correction of the foregoing errors; and b. Instruct the created Inventory Committee to comply with the guidelines on the preliminary activities prior to the conduct of inventory taking, reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records, disposition of non-existing/missing PPEs, derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without available records of accountability and other provisions under COA Circular No. 2020-006 to properly arrive at the correct balance of PPE resulting from the one-time cleansing.
observations.managementAction: The process of cleansing is on-going. Buildings transferred to Barangays are still found in the RPCPPE and there are still machineries with a unit value of less than ₱15,000.00 included in the RPCPPE. Assets were transferred to various Government Agencies. The Office provided the list of Completed projects already recognized in the books. The comparison of GSO and Accounting Office's records are partially done prior to the conduct of inventory due to the voluminous assets of the City. On-going reconciliation.
observations.reason: On-going reconciliation. Buildings transferred to Barangays are still found in the RPCPPE and there are still machineries with a unit value of less than ₱50,000.00 included in the RPCPPE. The reconciliation is still pending due to voluminous assets of the City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that LCE direct the City Accountant to maintain special accounts for the CCH and CBR under the General Fund and prepare the financial statements in order to determine the financial condition and performance of the said LEEs.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant is coordinating with the accountant of CBR and CCH. The City Accountant started to identify the assets and liabilities attributable to CBR and CCH for the preparation of financial statements of the said LEEs.
observations.reason: Ongoing establishment of new accounting system. No special account maintained in the FS. For further validation next year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the BAC to comply with the required posting of the NOA and NTP of all awarded contracts at the PhilGEPS portal within the reglementary period in compliance with Sections 37.1.6 and 37.4.2 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to ensure that the public is provided with the necessary information on a timely manner thus, encouraging the latter to participate in the effective monitoring of the implementation of projects of the City Government.
observations.managementAction: BAC posted NOA and/or NTP after all required documents have been submitted by the contractors. The NOA and/or NTP of at least 71 awarded contracts were posted at PhilGEPS beyond the reglementary period.
observations.reason: The NOA and/or NTP of at least 71 awarded contracts were posted beyond the reglementary period. Reiterated in Part II under Observation No. 9.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the PMC to inspect projects undertaken by administration to ensure immediate correction of detected defects/deficiencies, if any, in compliance with Section 5.1 of the DILG MC No. 2019-188. We also recommended that Management instruct the City Engineer to comply with the required signboards for projects constructed by administration in adherence to Section 2.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2013-004 to ensure publicity of all projects of the City Government and consequently encourage the public to participate in the monitoring of the implementation of these projects.
observations.managementAction: The PMC inspected three City projects undertaken during the year. Signboards were installed for projects which have undergone bidding. Signboards for 17 projects constructed by administration were not installed at their respective locations.
observations.reason: There were 14 projects undertaken by administration that were not inspected by the PMC. Signboards for 17 projects constructed 'by administration' were not installed at their respective locations. Reiterated in Part II under Observation No. 8.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Treasurer both agreed to exercise prudence in the utilization of the available resources, ensure that only highly necessary and legal expenditures are being paid, and fund held in trust be used only for their intended purposes.

We recommended further to which the Municipal officials and employees approved to observe austerity measures on its operation to address the cash deficit.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to verify and validate all dormant cash advances and other receivable accounts which remained outstanding for more than 10 years and determine whether the accountable officers are still in the service and there is possibility of liquidation of their accounts. Otherwise, the Municipal Accountant must recommend to the Municipal Mayor to file a request for authority to write-off dormant accounts with the COA following the requirements under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.reason: A request for write-off was made; however, it lacked supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality refrain from charging wages and meals from 20 percent Development Fund and other expenditure items not allowable to be charged against the fund and adhere to the provision of Item 3.2.5 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020.
observations.reason: Due to budget constraints, wages for garbage collectors were still charged against the 20% development fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant process and release funds to legitimate NGOs/POs with complete supporting documents, and regularly monitor project implementation as well as liquidation of funds as stipulated in the Memorandum of Agreement.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has already issued a demand letter to Cogon Farmers Association.
observations.reason: Still not settled as of December 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality fast track the immediate transfer of the titles of the lands in the name of the Municipality of Candoni to establish rightful ownership.
observations.managementAction: Municipal officials are exerting efforts to complete the titling of parcels of land owned by the Municipality.
observations.reason: Limited budget had prevented immediate complete processing for all parcels of land.

4.5 We recommended that the LCE require the City Accountant to provide
sufficient disclosure of the Investment Property account in the NFS.

4.6 In the letter reply of the City Accountant and during the exit conference, he
indicated that their office will ensure sufficient disclosure of the Investment
Property account in the NFS for the upcoming reporting periods.

5.8 We recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant to use the
suggested account titles in recording similar transactions as specified in the

5.9 The City Accountant stated that they will use the suggested account titles to
record similar transactions in the succeeding reporting periods. Moreover, they

to be used in recording transactions in the book of accounts.

1.8. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile the RPT and SET
Receivable with the Deferred RPT/SET Income accounts, confirm correct
amounts, and record the necessary adjusting entries to affected accounts
while properly documenting the process.

1.9. Henceforth, in compliance with NGAS, we recommended and the Municipal
Accountant agreed to prepare the JEV at the beginning of the year to record
the setting up of RPT and SET Receivable accounts and the corresponding
Deferred RPT or SET Income to present fairly their balances in the Statement
of Financial Position at year-end.

2.9. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the Municipal
Treasurer to intensify the collection of local taxes to reinforce the cash
position and enhance financial capability.

2.10. We further recommended and the Municipal Finance Committee agreed to
prepare a cash flow forecast to recommend to the Local Chief Executive the
matching of expected collections with disbursement schedules to avoid
overdrawing.

2.11. We also recommended and the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal
Treasurer, together with Municipal officials and employees, agreed to
exercise prudence in the utilization of the available resources that only highly
necessary and legal expenditures will be paid until such time as the cash
deficit is minimized or eliminated.

3.10. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a) Require the persons accountable to immediately settle unliquidated cash
advances amounting to ₱7.793 million according to existing regulations on the
settlement of cash advances.
b) Refrain from granting additional cash advances to employees and officers
with unliquidated cash advances, and to strictly comply with the guidelines in
the granting, utilization, and liquidation of the same.

4.7. We recommended and the LGU personnel in charge of property management
agreed to continue its sound practices to reinforce the LGU’s commitment to
financial integrity and compliance with applicable regulations.

5.7. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the GAD Focal
Person to ensure that all GAD-funded activities are supported with detailed
project proposals and training designs that demonstrate gender
responsiveness and guarantee the proper utilization of GAD resources.

5.8. We further recommended and the management agreed that all expenditures
in the GAD Plan and Budget be thoroughly reviewed to confirm their
alignment with existing gender issues of the Municipality in accordance with
existing rules and regulations.

6.11. We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the local
HRMO to formulate a uniform list of procedures in the recruitment of JO
workers to ensure that the hiring process is free from bias, including the
assigning of wage rates. A separate list of procedures shall also be created
for the evaluation of the accomplishments of JO workers for a certain period
to ensure that performance is commensurate with pay.

6.12. We further recommended and management agreed that the assessment and
evaluation of the existing manpower complement to fill up vacant plantilla
positions be conducted in order to minimize the hiring of JO employees.

7.8. We recommended and the Local School Board, thru the focal person, be
required to submit the quarterly and annual SEF Utilization Reports within
the period required to the DepEd Central Office to ensure transparency and
accountability. We further recommended the establishment of a monitoring
system to track the timely preparation and submission of reports to concerned
offices within the prescribed period in adherence to existing rules and
regulations.

10.5. We recommended and management agreed to continue the effective
utilization of the 20% Development Fund, along with its proper management
of funds for proposed projects, to maximize benefits for constituents and
enhance overall fund utilization.

12.5. We recommended and the officials of the Municipality agreed to maintain
effective control mechanisms for PS expenditures and continuously adhere
to established rules and regulations.

observations.managementAction: The Office of the SP coordinated with the SP Engineering Office to get the locations of the School Buildings and other relevant information and SP resolution was issued. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC carefully verify and review the contractors' statement of all ongoing contracts to detect misrepresentations which could possibly affect the determination of the bidders’ responsiveness to the bidding requirements pursuant to Section 34.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to reduce the risk that the projects will be awarded to bidders who may not satisfactorily complete the projects within the contract duration.
observations.managementAction: The TWG/BAC reviewed the contractors' Statement of All Ongoing Contracts. There were no inconsistencies noted in the bidders’ Statement of All Ongoing Contracts.
observations.reason: There were no inconsistencies noted in the bidders’ Statement of All Ongoing Contracts. The TWG/BAC reviewed the Statement of All Ongoing Contracts and findings were addressed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management adopt the calibrated actions provided under GPPB Circular No. 03-2019 at the earliest possible time in response to the delay in the implementation of infrastructure projects and initiate termination of contracts with negative slippage of at least 10 percent if contract time has already expired and contracts with negative slippage of at least 15% while the project is still on-going to protect the interest of the City Government and its constituents.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineering Office notified the contractors who incurred negative slippages requiring them to submit catch-up programs. Notices were sent to the contractors requiring them to submit catch-up programs.
observations.reason: Notices were sent to the contractors who incurred negative slippages requiring them to submit catch-up programs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to submit the required supporting documents prescribed by COA Circular No. 2012-001 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 to ascertain the validity and propriety of transactions.
observations.managementAction: The required supporting documents were submitted.
observations.reason: The supporting documents were submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the City Treasurer and City Accountant to immediately return to the source agencies the unspent balances of all funds received by the City, which purposes had already been served pursuant to Item 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013 thus, augmenting the funds of the government for the implementation of programs and projects.
observations.managementAction: The CTO and City Treasurer and City Accountant prepared the documents for the return of unspent balances and the unspent balances were returned to the source agencies on August and September, 2024.
observations.reason: Unspent funds were returned to the source agencies on August and September, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC and its TWG to perform in-depth post-qualification process of infrastructure projects subject for bidding to include the validation and verification of the competence and sufficiency of bidders’ personnel and availability of equipment units to be used in the contracts in compliance with Section 34.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to ensure that the key personnel and equipment are not concurrently assigned or pledged to two or more contracts and that the bidders meet the minimum equipment requirement, thus avoiding delay in project accomplishment and/or substandard quality of work.
observations.managementAction: The BAC verified the bidders' compliance with the requirements and conditions as specified in the bidding documents. The BAC through resolution established a BAC-TWG composed of Technical Persons to administer the Bid Evaluation and Post-Qualification. The BAC-TWG performed in-depth qualification and validation to the submitted documents. The BAC conducts validation in other agencies/on-site validation of offices.
observations.reason: Verified that projects from June to September 18, 2025 have no overlapping implementation period were awarded to bidders with the same personnel and/or equipment assigned to the contracts and no contracts were awarded to bidders who did not meet the minimum equipment requirements. The BAC-TWG performed in-depth qualification and validation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the implementing offices to review and update their PPMPs at least once every six months or as often as may be required by the HoPE and submit the same to the BAC Secretariat for consolidation into an updated APP to be submitted to the GPPB, pursuant to Section 7 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to ensure that all procurements are in accordance with the approved APP and are consistent with the duly approved budget of the procuring entity.
observations.managementAction: PPMPs were updated to reflect the revised schedule of bidding activities for some projects of the City. The revised schedule of bidding activities for some projects of the City were in the updated PPMPs.
observations.reason: The revised schedule of bidding activities for some projects of the City were updated in the PPMPs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to withhold the required amount of retention money on all payments to suppliers relative to the procurement of goods in the absence of bank guarantee in compliance with Section 62.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to oblige the suppliers to undertake necessary corrective actions on manufacturing defects, thus additional expenses for the repair of any defects of the goods delivered would be avoided.
observations.managementAction: The required amount of retention money is deducted/withheld automatically in the payments to suppliers of goods as reflected in the Disbursement Vouchers. Retention money is deducted/withheld automatically in the payments to suppliers of goods and it is released after the lapse of the warranty period, in the case of expendable supplies, the retention money is released after consumption.
observations.reason: Retention money is deducted/withheld automatically in the payments to suppliers of goods as reflected in the Disbursement Vouchers and released after the lapse of the warranty period or after consumption in the case of expendable supplies.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to remit collections in accordance with prescribed rules and regulations.

We further recommend and the Municipal Treasurer also agreed to implement robust controls and monitoring mechanisms to ensure timely and accurate remittance of collections. This includes regular reconciliations between collection records and bank deposits to identify any discrepancies or delays promptly.

We also recommended and the Management agreed that personnel involved in the collection processes are properly informed of prescribed policies and procedures including the importance of timely remittance in cash flow management and financial reporting.
observations.managementAction: the Municipal Treasurer agreed to remit collections in accordance with prescribed rules and regulations. The Municipal Treasurer also agreed to implement robust controls and monitoring mechanisms to ensure timely and accurate remittance of collections.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accountant prepare journal entries, correct the balances of the affected accounts, and reconcile the balance with the cash book of the accountable officer. Henceforth, thorough review of journal entries at year-end be performed so as to properly record transactions in the correct accounts to present fairly the financial statements.

We recommended and LGU officials agreed that the remaining balance of ₱1.463 million be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund (GF) pursuant to prescribed rules and regulations including fund transfer from Trust Fund, where these remaining balances are booked, to the GF. Subsequently, those funds that pertained to PDAF should be remitted or returned to the DOF thru the Bureau of Treasury.

We recommended and the Municipal officials agreed that the Municipal Health Program Coordinator at the Municipal Health Office furnish the Municipal Accountant with the approved detailed list of allocated vaccines received and utilized by the Municipality, and to recognize in the books the donations in accordance with existing regulations.
observations.managementAction: the Municipal Health Program Coordinator at the Municipal Health Office furnish the Municipal Accountant with the approved detailed list of allocated vaccines received and utilized by the Municipality, and to recognize in the books the donations in accordance with existing regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to monitor the completeness of the DVs from initial receipt until after recording in the journals in order to timely submit all DVs with complete supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor. observations.managementAction: the Municipal Accountant agreed to monitor the completeness of the DVs from initial receipt until after recording in the journals and to immediately submit the disbursement vouchers and the corresponding supporting documents of the 59 checks issued totaling ₱5.603 million to the Office of the Auditor.

This should be done prior to conducting the inventory, in accordance with Paragraph 6.1 of COA Circular No. 2020-006, to easily identify the items not recorded in either set of records.
2.8   Further, we recommended that Management direct the GSO to reconcile the inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records and prepare the required lists or reports to be submitted to the Accounting Office for recording in the books pursuant to Paragraph 6.3 of the same circular, to establish the correct balances of PPE accounts.

3.8 We recommended that the BAC carefully verify and validate the contractors’ statement of all ongoing contracts to detect misrepresentations which could possibly affect the determination of the bidders’ responsiveness to the bidding requirements pursuant to Section 34.3 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to reduce the risk that the projects will be awarded to bidders who may not satisfactorily complete the projects within the contract duration.

4.9  We recommended that Management adopt the calibrated actions provided under GPPB Circular No. 03-2019 at the earliest possible time in response to the delay in the implementation of infrastructure projects and initiate termination of contracts with negative slippage of at least 10 percent if contract time has already expired and contracts with negative slippage of at least 15% while the project is still on-going to protect the interest of the City Government and its constituents.

5.6 We recommended that Management direct the City Treasurer to provide the City Accountant with the certified list of taxpayers and taxes due based on the RPTAR at the beginning of each year. This list will be used by the City Accountant to set-up RPT and SET receivables during the year pursuant to Section 20, Volume 1 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, to ensure reliable balances of the said accounts in the financial statements as of year-end.

6.7 We recommended that the LCE instruct the BAC and the BAC Secretariat to ensure submission of copies of all contracts with the corresponding supporting documents to the Auditor within five working days from the execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001.
6.8 We also recommended that Management forfeit the bid security or enforce the bid securing declaration if the bidder is unable to sign the contract and submit the documents that form part of the contract within 10 calendar days from receipt of the NOA.

7.6 We recommended that Management require the City Accountant to submit the required supporting documents prescribed by COA Circular No. 2012-001 pursuant to Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 to ascertain the validity and propriety of transactions.

8.4 We recommended that Management instruct the City Treasurer and City Accountant to release the RPT share for the 42 barangays within five (5) days after the end of each quarter, in accordance with Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160. This would provide the barangays with additional funds for the implementation of their PPAs.

9.7 We recommended that Management instruct the City Human Resource and Management Office (CHRMO) to immediately update membership profile of the employees to reflect the new salary rate, employment status, position or any other important information by submitting the required forms to the GSIS and PHIC and furnish copy of these forms to the Accounting Office.
9.8 We also recommended that Management direct the Accounting Office to determine employees with unremitted contributions and overpayments and subsequently remit payments already due to GSIS, HDMF and PHIC and/or refund erroneously withheld payments to employees.
9.9 Furthermore, the Accounting Office should require the necessary forms before updating salaries or including new employees in the payroll to avoid the accumulation of unreconciled balances of the Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG and Due to PhilHealth accounts. Correspondingly, the employees will not incur any issues when claiming their benefits.

10.5 We recommended that Management instruct the City Treasurer and City Accountant to immediately return to the source agencies the unspent balances of all funds received by the City, which purposes had already been served pursuant to Item 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013 thus, augmenting the funds of the government for the implementation of programs and projects.

11.8 We recommended that the HoA instruct the City Accountant to:
Submit the supporting documents for the grant of the said cash advance to the Office of the Auditor in a sealed envelope with a visible label “CONFIDENTIAL – For the COA ATL Only” and with a covering transmittal letter detailing the submissions;
Restore the cash advance in the books by debiting ₱100,000.00 to Advances to Special Disbursing Officer and crediting Prior Period Adjustment in the same amount; and
Record the liquidation of the cash advance in the books upon receipt of the transmittal letter duly received by the ICFAU staff.

12.8 We recommended that Management instruct the implementing offices to prepare the POWs, technical specifications and other necessary documents prior to the inclusion of these PPAs in the budget to avoid delays in the implementation of projects.
12.9 We also recommended that Management instruct the implementing offices to fast track the execution/completion of the unimplemented projects and/or evaluate the viability of the same for appropriate realignment thus, the benefits which could be derived from the completed projects would be provided timely for the improvement of the City constituents’ economic well-being.
12.10 Finally, we recommended that Management require the CPDC, in coordination with other implementing offices to prepare a monitoring report for all projects of the City to ensure that all development projects of the City are implemented in accordance with the approved budgets.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the City Treasurer to prepare the required Schedule of Unreleased Commercial Checks per fund as of year-end for submission to the City Accountant as basis for the preparation of adjusting entry to restore the balance of cash and recognize the appropriate liability accounts, in compliance with the provisions of Section 352 of the GAM for LGUs.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to recognize impairment loss by debiting Impairment Loss – Other Receivables and crediting Allowance for Impairment - Other Receivables each by ₱1,881,640.00 to fairly present the book value of affected accounts.

We further recommend that Management direct the concerned City employees to employ necessary measures for the collection of these receivables. If these remain uncollected despite employing all possible remedies, the Management may request write-off following the procedures provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management instruct:
The employee in charge of receiving donated items to furnish a copy of the Invoice Receipt or any other proof of receipt to the Accounting Office; and
The City Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entry to recognize the donated equipment by debiting the Medical Equipment account in the amount of ₱13,800,000.00 and crediting Grants and Donations in Kind by the same amount.

We recommended that Management Instruct the City Engineer to provide the City Budget Officer and the City Accountant with a copy of the SWA/Certificate of Completion of the unbilled completed projects as basis for the recognition of PPE and Accounts Payable as of year-end; and the City Accountant to draw a JEV with  the following adjusting journal entries:
Flood Control System		       991,673.35
Markets				       6,793,252.07
Accounts Payable		       7,784,925.42

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to adhere to the accounting policy in the recording of PPE expenditures pursuant to the provisions of the GAM in order to fairly present the account balances. Accordingly, prepare the necessary journal entry to correct the error in the books of accounts, as follows:
Other Structures                                        627,553.28
Buildings				        2,532,151.36
Markets					        1,565,534.88
ICT Equipment				       598,500.00
RM – Buildings and Other Structures      	4,725,239.52
RM – Machinery and Equipment			   598,500.00

We recommended that the Management instruct the Property Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of physical inventory of the PPE and submit the result thereof to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year in order to provide basis of reliance on the recorded balances.

We recommended that Management instruct the CPDC to:
Coordinate with CAAP regarding the conduct of additional tests to be performed as required in its previously issued Technical Evaluation Report;
Refrain from allocating and disbursing additional government funds for the continued acquisition of lot until such time that a MOA is entered into by the City Government and the CAAP; and
Immediately, formulate an alternative action plan in case the proposed airport will not materialize to prevent idling of the acquired lots considering the large scale of amount involved.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Engineer to accomplish and update signboards/billboards to encourage public participation in monitoring timeliness and completeness of the implemented projects, thus promoting good governance through transparency and accountability.

We recommended that Management direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to comply with the publication of the required information and documents of the City Government’s succeeding emergency purchases at the GPPB online portal, relative to the utilization of the remaining balance of the Bayanihan Fund amounting ₱11,066,683.14 to enhance transparency and enforce accountability.

We recommended that Management adopt the calibrated actions provided under GPPB Circular No. 03-2019 in response to the delay in the construction of the CCH Annex and in the implementation of other infrastructure projects to protect the interest of the City Government and its constituents.

We recommended that Management instruct the City General Services Officer to immediately dispose the unserviceable properties with a book value of ₱17,521,697.14 in accordance with the procedures provided under COA Circular No. 92-386, thus:
File an application for the disposal of unserviceable properties with the COA. Said application shall be supported with Inventory and Inspection Report for Unserviceable Property (IIRUP);
Furnish the City Accountant with a copy of the accomplished IIRUP as basis in dropping their carrying values; and
If said properties were determined by COA to be without value, see that these are destroyed in their presence. If these were determined by COA with value, then initiate public bidding with a floor price that was formulated by the BAC based on the recommendation of the former.

We recommended that Management require all City employees with work-from-home arrangements from May 8, 2020 to December 31, 2020 to submit their accomplishment reports verified by their respective department heads as proof of rendered services to establish the validity of paid salaries and wages.

We recommended that Management require the contractor to undertake the necessary repair of the damaged portion of the FMR at Sitio Kinamantirisan-Oaig Daya, and FMR at Purok 1, 6 & 7-Parioc Primero at his own expense to prevent further deterioration of the concrete pavement and to prolong the useful life of the road.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly comply with the implementation of the DBM-approved purposes on the utilization of the released funds in order that tobacco farmers would not be denied of the benefits that would be derived from the livelihood projects as provided under Section 2 of the RA No. 7171; and
Instruct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entry to correct the understatement of biological assets and overstatement of expenses by debiting Breeding Stocks and crediting Donations each by ₱2,840,000.00 to present fairly the affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the concerned employees of the CHO to submit the lists of recipients/proof of acknowledgment by recipients indicating therein the name of medicine and quantity received to properly account all issuances made to various beneficiaries from the procured drugs and medicines worth ₱2,392,905.93; and
Instruct the concerned offices to record the receipts of donated drugs and medicines in the stock cards and in the books of accounts.

We recommended that Management require the City Treasurer to supervise the LO in order for the latter to closely monitor the remittances of the two field collectors to ensure that all collections are turned over daily based on the cut-off time established, in compliance with Section 63 of the GAM for LGUs.  Moreover, in order to curtail such practice, Management institute disciplinary action and/or impose other sanctions, if warranted.

We recommended that Management instruct the CDRRMO, in order for the beneficiaries to have a safer and more convenient place to live in, to expedite the completion and turnover of the 42 housing units to the intended beneficiaries through:
Prioritization on the installation of windows;
Submission of a copy of the subdivision plan to the ISECO as the basis for the provision of electrical services to the resettlement area; and
Follow-up communication with the CWD regarding the installation of water lines from the overhead water tank to the individual housing units.

We recommended that Management instruct the CVO to conduct regular monitoring as regards the condition/s of the breeding stocks and their offspring and provide them immediate and proper medication if found ill to ensure the success and continuity of the project and more so, achieving the objective of upgrading the economic status of beneficiaries who are involved in farming and livestock production.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Foundation, Inc. to remit the amount of ₱3,613,854.14 representing the balance of unremitted net receipts from operations of the CCH for the period 2005 to 2020; and
Instruct the City Accountant to verify the submitted financial statements of the hospital for the previous years of operations and ensure that only cash operating expenses are deducted from gross receipts before arriving at the correct mount for remittance to the City Government.

We recommended that Management, after expiration of the MOA with the CCCAAFI, subject the management of the CBR operations to a competitive public bidding whereby interested parties will base their monthly rental fee quotations on a floor price commensurate to the existing facilities of the resort. Consider a short term contract with the winning bidder to determine the viability of its terms and conditions.  Upon expiration of the contract, a usual public bidding will take place after an offer for renewal to the lessee is rejected, thus, CBR’s cost-effective management would be realized.
The report does not contain an explicit "We recommended" paragraph immediately following this observation within Part II.

We recommended that the Accounting Office maintain property
ledger cards in accordance with Section 120 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs
and reconcile the same with the Property Cards maintained by the GSO as
initial steps in gradually addressing the discrepancies between the historical
value of the assets and the latest inventory reports.

We recommended that the GSO hasten the tracing of missing
unserviceable properties and request for the disposal of unserviceable
properties at hand, by submitting the Inventory and Inspection Report of
Unserviceable Properties supported with pictures to the Auditor pursuant to
Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445.

We likewise recommended that the GSO impose proper control
measures to ensure that all property accountabilities are settled prior to
retirement/separation of city employees or officials from government service
to prevent occurrence of similar situations where derecognition for fair
presentation of the asset balances cannot be recorded pending final disposal.

We recommended that the City Engineering Office notify the
Accounting Office of the projects already completed by furnishing copies of
Certificate of Completion for the reclassification of CIP accounts to the
appropriate PPE accounts and recording of the unbilled portion of contract
costs.

We recommended that the City Accountant use the date of completion
of the infrastructure projects in computing for depreciation expenses instead
of the date of payment in order to arrive at the correct amount of
depreciation and to reflect the correct book value of the PPEs in the financial
statements.

We recommended that Management observe the proper accounting
method in recording receipt and distribution of inventory items, conduct
regular physical inventory of supplies and submit RPCI semestrally, and
maintain Stock Cards and Stock Ledger Cards for all supplies and materials.
It is further recommended that the Accounting Office record the
necessary adjusting entry to recognize the undistributed inventories as of
December 31, 2019:

Other Supplies and Materials for Distribution 425,100.00
Other Supplies and Materials Inventory 162,000.00
Prior Year Adjustment 587,100.00

We recommended that Management deliver the procured inventory
items to the intended recipients the soonest possible time following the actual
delivery to serve the purpose of the procurement consistent with the
principles of efficiency and effectiveness in government operations.

We recommended that Management:
a. Monitor availability of drugs and medicines and medical, dental
and laboratory supplies by preparing the SCs and SLCs that
would aid in making timely purchases;
b. Instruct the CHO employees in charge of receiving donated items
from other agencies to furnish a copy of the invoice receipt or
other proof of receipt to the Accounting Office. The City
Accountant should then recognize the receipt of donated items in
the books of accounts at cost or appraised value; and
c. Strictly issue drugs and medicines to patients based on doctor’s
prescription.

We recommended that the City Treasurer provide the City
Accountant, an updated Certified List of Real Property Taxpayers showing
the name of taxpayers at the beginning of the year and the amount due and
collectible for the year based on the RPTAR which will be the basis in setting
up RPT and SET receivables.

Also, the City should update their accounting system to comply with
the adoption of Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards which would
aide them in maintaining a more accurate record of RPUs.

We recommended that the City Treasurer maintain a complete list of
real property tax delinquencies and collect the same notwithstanding the
provisions of Section 270 of the Local Government Code.

We recommended that the OIC-City Assessor prepare a schedule of
fair market values for the different classes of real property situated in the
City for the enactment of ordinance by the Sangguniang Panlungsod
pursuant to Sections 212 and 219 of the LGC.

We recommended that the City Accountant allow the grant of cash
advances only when the previous advances given to an accountable officer
were liquidated pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 to avoid the
overlapping and accumulation of cash in the custody of the officer which
could possibly be misused/lost.

We recommended that the Management secure the needed documents
as soon as possible for the titling of the lots already paid by the City
Government to avoid possible third party claims and land disputes.

We recommended that Management strictly observe prudence on the
use of government funds for meals and snacks by formulating a method to
obtain reasonable estimates of the number of attendees on scheduled events
and ensure authenticity of the attached supporting documents.

Also, we recommended that the City Accountant use the correct
entries in recording expenses.

Further, in order to avoid incurring expenditures beyond the
budgeted amount, we further recommended that the City Government limit
Representation Expenses to events deemed most necessary for its efficient
operations.

We recommended that the Gasoline Control Officers:
a. Ensure that the driver’s trip tickets are properly and fully
accomplished to enable the assigned driver/s to prepare the Report
of Official Travels; and
b. Prepare the Report of Fuel Consumption monthly for the Audit
Team to ascertain the reasonableness of the fuel consumption of
the City Government pursuant to COA Circular No. 77-61.

We recommended that the City Accountant closely examine the
documents attached to the disbursement vouchers to properly record
gasoline issued to barangays for their operations as Donations for the fair
presentation of the account balances in the Financial Statements of the City
Government.

We recommended that the Management complete the requirements
for their system upgrade and request for the update of their e-NGAS
accounting system from the COA IT Office to facilitate the preparation of
financial statements in accordance with Section 41 (2) of P.D. No. 1445. We
also recommended that the City Accountant strictly transmit disbursement
vouchers to the Auditor within the period prescribed under COA Circular
No. 95-006 for the timely review of the transactions of the City Government.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive assess the workloads
of personnel currently involved in government procurement for possible
adjustments allowing them to be on a “jury duty” without sacrificing their
regular functions.

We further recommended that the BAC and the BAC Secretariat
ensure submission of copies of all contracts with the corresponding
supporting documents to the Auditor within five (5) working days from the
execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that Management consider the following:
a. Advise the Human Resource Management Officer to be religious
in deducting the leaves of absences of the concerned employees
from their accumulated leave credits earned;
b. Require those concerned employees who file their leaves to
properly accomplish the application for leaves indicating correctly
therein the required data and information;
c. Advise the heads of Offices that approval of their DTRs should be
done by the City Mayor and not by themselves;
d. Instruct the employees to be responsible in the preparation and
submission of their own DTRs with complete supporting
documents, e.g. travel orders, applications for leave, pass
slips/locator slips, if applicable, to justify the correctness of
records of services rendered by them;
e. Require the heads of every offices/departments to verify the
correctness of all entries in the DTRs and completeness of
documents to support the same before their approval and
attachment of signatures;
f. Require the Secretary assigned in every department to check the
accuracy and completeness of the documents attached to the DTRs
submitted for the final review and approval by the Human
Resource Management Officer; and
g. Require each employee to strictly observe the use of finger scan
biometrics to justify their attendance.

We recommended that the City Accountant properly verify the
entries in the daily time records of the employees and secure the attendance
to sessions of the Sanggunian Panglunsod Members to determine their leaves
of absences as basis in the computation of their RATA. It is further
recommended that Management strictly observe the guidelines on the
payment of RATA specifically under Sections 7.1 and 7.5 of DBM LBC
No. 103 dated May 15, 2013 to avoid the incurrence of expenses and
processing of payments in excess of the actual entitlements.

We also recommended that the City Government enjoin the
Department Heads and SP Members whose names appear in the
attached schedules to refund the amount representing overpayment of said
allowances.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the concerned employee to report to the City Hall and
perform his duties and responsibilities in line with his plantilla
position for the benefit of the City Government and its
constituents;
b. Communicate to the Foundation that the City Government will be
limited to exercising its visitorial rights and that the Hospital
Management may need to engage the services of its own personnel
to take charge of managing its human resources; and
c. Ensure that both parties to the MOA comply with the provisions
which include that the Foundation shall pay for the salary of those
employees whose services were engaged by it.

We recommended that Management consider the more prudent
options without sacrificing efficiency and effectiveness in relation to the
specific purpose/s instead of purchasing costly models/brands for the
optimum utilization of government funds.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Treasurer:
a. To immediately initiate the disposal by submitting four (4) copies
of inventory and all the obsolete checks to the Office of the
Auditor for inspection and destruction pursuant to Section 99 of
GAAM, Volume I;
b. To ensure that destruction of obsolete accountable forms is made
in the presence of the Auditor; and
c. To avoid excessive requisitions of accountable forms in order not
to encounter similar problem of wasted resources due
obsolescence.

We recommended that the CPDC ensure the maximum utilization of
the 20% CDF by implementing priority development projects while
observing strict adherence with the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-
1 pertaining to the expenditures properly charged to the 20% CDF.

We also recommended that Management prioritize the conduct of
wind rose analysis, detailed engineering and feasibility studies, evaluation on
the effect on navigable airspace and review of land use and zoning/planning
ordinance as recommended by the CAAP and laid down on their Technical
Evaluation Report, not only to ensure viability of the airport project and
avoid future operational problems but also to support related expenditures.

We recommended that the CDRRMO include previous years’
unexpended DRRMF-MOOE and QRF in the CDRRMFIP using the
prescribed format. For the implemented projects which were not included in
the CDRRMFIP, submit the Appropriation Ordinance to ratify the funds
availability and utilization thereof.

We also recommended that the City Accountant ensure that the
utilization of the DRRMF is in accordance with COA Circular 2012-002.

We recommended that the City Accountant and the CDRRMO
prepare and submit the monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of
DRRMF to facilitate timely evaluation of the charges against the fund.

We recommended that the CDRRMO see to it that all projects are
efficiently and effectively implemented to realize the maximum benefits for
which the DRRMF was created.

We recommended that the Local School Board strictly prepare the
SEF budget in accordance with the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1,
s. 2017 and refrain from including expenses that are not in the list of
allowable charges against SEF to maximize the use of the fund.

We recommended that Management continue to exert best efforts to
be fully compliant with the provisions of RA 9003 and ensure 100%
establishment of MRFs in the 42 barangays of the City by closely involving
the non-complying barangays in the strict implementation of the ten-year
SWMP.

We recommended that the Officials and employees involved in the
implementation of GAD programs/projects/activities see to it that the usage
of the fund is maximized for the purpose it was created.

We recommended that Management stop granting financial
assistance directly to farmers or through cooperatives and consider other
livelihood programs that could bring more tangible benefits to the farmers to
fully realize the objectives of the tobacco fund set forth under R.A. 7171.

We recommended that the implementing offices see to it that the
provisions in the Memorandum of Agreement are being complied with and
that the obligation of the recipients to provide reports is always included
therein.

We recommended that the City Accountant see to it that all necessary
documents are attached to the disbursement vouchers not only in pursuance
to Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 but most importantly, to support the validity
of the transactions and allow timely evaluation thereof during post audit.

We recommended that the BAC emphasize the completeness of
disclosures during pre-bid conferences to ensure reliability of information to
be used as basis for the award to winning bidders.

We also recommended that the TWG carefully verify and validate the
contractors’ statement of all ongoing contracts to detect misrepresentations
which could possibly affect the determination of the bidders’ responsiveness
to the procurement project and the recommendation for award.

We recommended that the Engineering Office conscientiously prepare
the design plans to avoid or minimize project suspensions; conduct stricter
supervision on the projects being implemented; and act promptly on the
issues or constraints that cause delay. We further recommended that
Management impose the payment of actual liquidated damages and/or
institute the necessary remedies provided under RA No. 9184 to protect the
interest of the City Government and its constituents.

We recommended that the implementing units/inspectorate team
henceforth prepare the punch-list required under Item 7, Annex E of the
2016 revised IRR of RA 9184 to ensure full completion of projects and
correction of work deficiencies prior to final turn-over. We recommended
further that Management intiate necessary actions to make the contractors
liable for the cost deficiencies in the civil works.

We recommended that Management require the contractor to
undertake the necessary repair of the longitudinal crack to prevent further
deterioration of the concrete pavement and to prolong the useful life of the
road asset.

We recommended that Management require the Engineering Office to
immediately submit the construction plans/diagrams or any equivalent
document to support the pay items and actual accomplishments for the two
projects.

We recommended that the Management ensure a sustained capacity
building for the members of the BAC for increased procurement proficiency.
We recommended further that Management discontinue the practice of
paying compensation for the observers’ attendance to the procurement
proceedings considering that this has no legal basis.

We recommended that Management exercise its visitorial rights over
the affairs of the CCH by assigning an internal audit unit to conduct audit of
the operations of the CCH and verify the reported net receipts to ensure that
the correct amount will be remitted by the foundation to the City Government
and to establish reliability of the financial information submitted to the LGU.

We recommended that the Management immediately enforce the
settlement of the audit suspensions to forestall the issuance of Notice of
Disallowance and the settlement of the disallowance by the person/s liable as
prescribed under Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct a complete physical inventory of all its PPE to facilitate the verification of the existence and actual condition of these assets. We also recommended that the GSO reconcile their report with the records of the Accounting Office to ensure that all properties of the City as appearing in the financial statements are duly accounted for.
observations.managementAction: The City GSO conducted physical count of its properties during the year.
observations.reason: The RPCPPE for the year was submitted to the Office. The inventory report submitted did not however reconcile with the amounts appearing on the financial statements. Discussed fully in Observation No. 1, pages 30-32 of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City GSO facilitate the disposal of the unserviceable properties of the City by preparing the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties and submit the same to the Auditor for appropriate actions. Upon disposal, the City Accountant should drop the cost of the unserviceable properties to present fairly the amount of Property, Plant and Equipment in the books.
observations.reason: No disposal of unserviceable properties was done during the year. The Audit Team did not receive any request for inspection of unserviceable properties for disposal during the year. Reiterated in Observation No. 1, pages 32-33 of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management facilitate the titling of all of land owned by the City Government to avoid dispute of ownership/exposure to third party claims.
observations.managementAction: The City Assessor’s Office processed some lots and found out that some lots cannot be processed yet due to incomplete documents needed for the processing of land titles, despite that some of these untitled lots were paid several years ago.
observations.reason: Verified with the Assessor’s Office that the titling of twelve (12) lots were processed and some lots could not be processed yet due to needed documents not available. Reiterated in Observation No. 5, pages 45-46 of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant prepare an adjusting entry to recognize in the books at least 75% of the Public Infrastructures, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-004 dated September 30, 2016.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant transferred in the books all the Public Infrastructures recorded in the registry.
observations.reason: Based on our review of the registry, the public infrastructures recorded in the registry were all transferred in the books as of December 31, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the City Treasurer’s Office to intensify efforts to collect the receivables. Accounts of recipients whose whereabouts could no longer be traced and those who are already dead may be subjected for write-off by the agency upon completion of the required documents.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer’s Office collected some of the receivables upon payment of rent and renewal of their business permits.
observations.reason: Based on review of General Ledgers, some other receivables from prior years were collected during the year. However, most of the long outstanding receivables remain uncollected as of December 31, 2019. No request for write-off of receivables was received by the Audit Team during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Agriculturist and the City Veterinarian to make payments for the breeding stocks since they were the direct recipients of the same. In like manner, if settlements have been made, the Accounting Office should make appropriate entries in the books for the derecognition of the cost of breeding stocks in order to reflect correct financial statements of the agency.
observations.managementAction: The concerned employees paid the breeding stocks which were already derecognized in the books. The City Accountant derecognized the breeding stocks in the books through JEV-2019-12-007557 upon payment of P75,997.00 by the concerned employees.
observations.reason: Based on the General Ledger, it was verified that the breeding stocks which were already dead were derecognized in the books. The concerned employees paid the breeding stocks in the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant use the appropriate accounts in recording expenditures in order to present fairly the MOOE accounts in the financial statements.
observations.reason: The Other MOOE account was significantly reduced from P61,559,766.00 in 2018 to P28,121,569.48 in 2019. Post-audit of disbursements and JEVs showed that several expenses during the year were still improperly charged to Other MOOE account but overall the account balance was reduced.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Office of the City Engineer and Public Order and Safety Division to return the units of multi cab issued to them and distribute the same thereafter to the intended barangay beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: The two barangays were prioritized in the distribution of multi cabs by the City Government instead of retrieving the units which were already used by the City Engineering and Public Order and Safety Offices.
observations.reason: Post-audit of disbursements revealed that the procurement of two multi cabs by the City Government were issued to City offices instead of the intended recipient barangays. The intended barangays eventually received the multi cabs during the year; however some units were already used by City offices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the CDRRMO see to it that the projects included in the DRRM Plan are strictly implemented on time to help the communities prepare for calamities and build their resilience to disasters.
observations.reason: Management failed to report on several PPAs for DRRM during the year. Based on the review of the utilization of the fund, only 61.32% of the mitigation fund was utilized during the year. Reiterated in Observation No. 16.3, page 72 of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City use the exact lot area owned or tilled by the farmers as basis in the distribution of fertilizers and cash assistance and other goods to ensure that the beneficiaries are given only what they are entitled to receive. The City may use the lot area indicated in the tax declaration of the land owned or tilled by the intended beneficiaries instead of using estimates.
observations.managementAction: The City distributed fertilizers using the exact lot area distributed by the farmers.
observations.reason: Based on post-audit, the City distributed fertilizers to farmers based on lot area owned or tilled as provided by the farmers and supported by tax declarations in some instances. The fertilizers distributed amounted to P14,992,430.00 in December 2019 based on lot area tilled or owned.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee strictly follow the timeline indicated in the Annual Procurement Plan to avoid delay in the implementation of the projects and the City Engineering Office to strictly monitor the implementation of the infrastructure projects of the City. We also recommended that the Management enforce the collection of liquidated damages for the projects that were not completed on time.
observations.managementAction: Management failed to follow the procurement schedule of several projects. Liquidated damages were collected from erring contractors and Management assured they will be deducting the same upon final payment of delayed projects.
observations.reason: The City failed to follow the procurement schedule as some projects were not scheduled and significant delays were incurred. Liquidated damages were collected for some projects and Management assured collection upon final payment of delayed projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require contractors of infrastructure projects to have their construction and safety program approved by the Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE) and to secure applicable test analysis from DOST/BRS accredited laboratories to ensure the quality of the materials used in the construction of infrastructure projects.
observations.managementAction: Management submitted some certificates and results of test analysis which were attached to some contracts and/or disbursement vouchers.
observations.reason: The two contractors provided CSHP certificates and results of test analysis attached to some contracts and disbursement vouchers. Most contractors complied during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Engineer strictly monitor compliance of all contractors on the installation of signboards in compliance with COA Circular 2013-004. We further recommended that Management require the contractors to provide billboards otherwise, the contractor shall refund the cost of billboards if payments have already been made. If no payments have been made yet, the cost of the billboards shall be deducted from the total amount due to the contractor.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineering Office supervised the installation of signboards for the different infrastructure projects of the City.
observations.reason: Site inspection of some of the infrastructure projects supervised the installation of signboards. Some signboards were not already installed at the project location at the time of inspection.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant maintain a special account for the City of Candon Hospital and prepare the financial statements as required under Sections 105, 107 and 110 of NGAS Manual in order to determine the financial condition and performance of the said local economic enterprise.
observations.managementAction: Management failed to create a special account for the CCH.
observations.reason: The City failed to create a special account for the CCH. No separate account was maintained by the City Government for the operations of the CCH.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer immediately deposit to the Bureau of Treasury the unspent balances of all funds received from NGAs which purposes had already been served in compliance with Executive Order No. 431 dated May 30, 2005.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer facilitated immediate deposit of unspent balances to the Bureau of Treasury.
observations.reason: Post-audit of facilitated deposit and review of the ledger accounts revealed that unspent balances were immediately deposited to the Bureau of Treasury.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant make a thorough review of the computations on the claim of overtime pay of employees and ensure that these claims are in accordance with the policies and guidelines provided in the CSC-DBM Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2015 dated November 25, 2015 to avoid the incurrence of irregular expenses which are disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office thoroughly reviewed the computations for the overtime charges during the year.
observations.reason: Post-audit of disbursements during the year showed that the guidelines for the computation of overtime charges were strictly adhered to.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant strictly observe the timely submission of trial balances as required under Sections 70 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs to facilitate review of reports, otherwise, we will be compelled to recommend for the imposition of the necessary sanctions as provided by law.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office failed to submit trial balances within the required time.
observations.reason: Trial balances were still not submitted monthly to the Office during the year. Discussed in a similar observation, pages 51-52 of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City strictly follow the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and 96-010 to facilitate review of the contracts/purchase orders and to enable us to inspect the goods purchased immediately so that any defect or deficiency that may be noted thereon will be acted upon immediately.
observations.managementAction: Management entered into some lease agreements and submitted purchase orders within the required time. Most deliveries of goods purchased were not notified to the Auditor and delays extending to 896 days were incurred in the submission of contracts and supporting documents.
observations.reason: No special request for contracts/purchase orders and supporting documents caused delays. Delays extending to 896 days were recorded in the submission of contracts and supporting documents. Reiterated in Observation No. 9, pages 52-54 of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the CENRO Head to arrange the work schedule of employees efficiently and refrain from assigning people to perform waste segregation and recycling on the afternoon of Saturdays, Sundays and holidays and sell hollow blocks at a price to recover at least the cost of production.
observations.managementAction: The CENRO Head arranged a revised work schedule of employees assigned at the landfill. The revised schedule does not include waste segregation and recycling during the afternoon of weekends; activities during weekends were limited to collection of wastes.
observations.reason: Based on the review of the landfill, activities assigned at the landfill did not include segregation and recycling during the afternoon of Saturdays and Sundays. Weekend activities were limited to collection of wastes.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management consider weighing the cost and benefits that can be derived in purchasing its own equipment instead of paying rental fees every time there is a City event.
observations.managementAction: Management entered into a contract for the rental of lights and sound rental of lights and sound system covering various activities of the City after consideration of cost efficiency.
observations.reason: Post-audit of disbursements revealed that the City paid for rental of lights and sound system amounting to P1,195,075.50 compared to P979,807.50 in 2018. Management considered cost efficiency in entering into rental contracts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City require the Foundation to strictly observe the timely submission of financial statements and other required reports for the evaluation and monitoring of the performance of the economic enterprise and require the Manager of the foundation to submit an explanation on the collection of 5% management fee.
observations.managementAction: The foundation submitted only a profit and loss statement for the year. No justification was provided by the foundation on the 5% management fee. The 2019 financial report was submitted showing that the foundation no longer collected management fee during the year.
observations.reason: The Foundation submitted only a profit and loss statement for the year and no other required financial reports. The 2019 financial report submitted showed that the foundation no longer collected management fee during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City enter into lease agreements with the 83 stall occupants to facilitate collection and avoid possible loss of income through the City Treasurer issuing official receipts to acknowledge payment of rent, which shall serve as basis of the City Accountant to record it as actual income from rent. With regards to occupants who are paying cash tickets on a daily basis, we recommended that the Management employ measures to verify the correctness of the collectors’ logbooks.
observations.managementAction: Management entered into lease agreements with some occupants. However, no lease contracts were entered into for some small value agreements (i.e. Kalamayan Stand) which were entered into between the City Government and the lessees.
observations.reason: Lease contracts were entered into with some regular stall occupants. However, some small value lease agreements were entered into and recorded as cash tickets. Review of ledgers showed that some occupants were still paying daily cash tickets and measures to verify collectors' logbooks were not fully implemented.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Require the City Treasurer’s Office to submit the Report of Checks issued  and Report of Collections and Deposits daily to the Accounting Office pursuant to Section 43 on the Manual on NGAS and to facilitate submission to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period;

Instruct the City Accountant to record entries in the Journals on a daily basis or as soon as the financial documents are submitted by the City Treasurer’s Office to prevent delay in the preparation of the financial statements; and

Require the City Accountant to submit paid vouchers and supporting documents and trial balances within the prescribed period pursuant to COA Circular No. 95-006.
observations.managementAction: Updated submission of accounts.
observations.reason: Based on the transmittal letters of documents, some disbursement vouchers, financial reports, report of disbursements, and reports of checks issued were still not submitted on time.

CSC-DBM Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2015 dated November 25, 2015 to avoid the incurrence of irregular expenses which are disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive request the Sangguniang Panlungsod to pass a resolution donating the transferred properties to the recipient agencies as basis by the Accounting Office to derecognize the subject transferred properties from the books of accounts of the City.
